source_intake_record.raw_question_text: A chain with mass/length $=u$ hanging vertically from one end, where an upward force $F$ is applied to it, is lowered onto a table as shown in Fig. 1.103. Find the equation of motion for $h$, the height of the end above the table ( $h$ is the length of chain hanging freely).

candidate_problem_record.subject: 物理
candidate_problem_record.raw_question_text: A chain with mass/length $=u$ hanging vertically from one end, where an upward force $F$ is applied to it, is lowered onto a table as shown in Fig. 1.103. Find the equation of motion for $h$, the height of the end above the table ( $h$ is the length of chain hanging freely).
candidate_problem_record.multi_solution_policy_rationale: 该数据集保留多解潜力评估，但不默认强推多解 agent。

extraction_notes: image field contains a PIL object string rather than a file path or URL
metadata.reasoning_chain: Solution: As this problem involves variable mass it is more convenient to work with momentum. Consider the change of momentum of the chain during a time interval $t$ to $t+\Delta t$. If $h$ and $v$ are respectively the height and velocity of the freely-hanging portion of the chain at time $t$, its momentum is $\mu h v$ at $t$ and $\mu (h-\Delta h)(v+\Delta v)$ at $t+\Delta t$ (see Fig. 1.104). As a portion $\Delta h$ of the chain has reached the table and transferred a momentum $\approx \mu \Delta h v$ to the latter during the interval, the momentum theorem gives $\mu (h-\Delta h)(v+\Delta v)+\mu \Delta h v-\mu h v=(\mu h g-F) \Delta t$, or, retaining only the first-order terms, $\mu h \Delta v=(\mu h g-F) \Delta t$. With $\Delta t \rightarrow 0$, the above becomes $\mu h \dot{v}=\mu h g-F$. As $v=-\dot{h}$, we have the equation of motion $\ddot{h}=\frac{F}{\mu h}-g$.

Consider the change of momentum of the chain during a time interval $t$ to $t+\Delta t$. If $h$ and $v$ are respectively the height and velocity of the freely-hanging portion of the chain at time $t$, its momentum is $\mu h v$ at $t$ and $\mu (h-\Delta h)(v+\Delta v)$ at $t+\Delta t$ (see Fig. 1.104).
